title: The bookseller who knew which book you needed
summary: Reflect on a particular bookseller, librarian, or shop owner who recommended a book that changed something for you, and what that kind of personal recommendation offers that an algorithm cannot.
topic: A bookseller, librarian, or shopkeeper who recommended exactly the right book
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a bookseller, librarian, or shopkeeper who once recommended a book that mattered to you. Contrast their kind of recommendation with how an algorithm chooses books. Use the past perfect to set context, present perfect for reflection, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although' for concession.
hintStarters: I had walked into the shop with no idea what I was looking for, only a vague feeling that I needed something I would not have chosen for myself…
What an algorithm would have offered me, after years of tracking what I had bought, was probably…
Although it sounds like the sort of nostalgic story people tell about old shops, what happened that afternoon has stayed with me because…

hintVocab.meaningVi: lời gợi ý mang tính cá nhân
một cuốn tiểu thuyết ít người biết
đọc đúng được con người ai đó
nhẹ nhàng đẩy ai đó về phía
một lời gợi ý nhẹ nhàng

hintVocab.phrase: the right book at the right time
hintVocab.meaningVi: đúng cuốn sách vào đúng thời điểm

sampleText:
On a wet October afternoon in Lisbon, I walked into a tiny second-hand bookshop on Rua das Salgadeiras with no clear idea of what I was looking for. The owner, a man in his seventies named Joaquim, watched me browse for almost twenty minutes without saying anything. Eventually he stood up, walked to a shelf I had already passed twice, and handed me a slim novel by a Hungarian writer I had never heard of. He said, simply, 'I think today this is the book for you.' I had told him nothing about myself. I had not even paid for anything yet. Yet the book — about a translator who returns to a city she has not visited since her teenage years — landed in my hands at a moment when I had been thinking about almost exactly that subject for weeks.

What made the recommendation possible was something an algorithm, however sophisticated, cannot easily reproduce: Joaquim had watched me for twenty minutes. He had noticed which shelves I lingered at, which spines I touched and put back, the way I held a book before deciding against it. He had also been doing this work for forty years, in the same neighbourhood, with the same kind of customers. Although a recommendation engine knows what you have bought, it does not see your shoulders relax when you find a certain kind of cover. It does not register that you opened a book to the middle and read for thirty seconds before putting it down disappointed. It works only from the trail of decisions you have already made, and most of those decisions are not the interesting ones.

What I have come to understand, several years later, is that the best recommendations are not predictions of what you already want — they are gentle introductions to what you do not yet know you want. Joaquim handed me a book I would never have searched for, and an algorithm that watched my reading history would not have suggested it either, because nothing about my past purchases pointed in that direction. However, the bookshop survived on this kind of judgement until it closed in 2022. I still think about it on afternoons when I find myself scrolling through a list of recommended titles that all somehow look the same. A shop with one person paying attention to one customer at a time is slower, smaller, and probably uneconomic. It is also, occasionally, the only thing that can give you a book you did not know to ask for.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I think today this is the book for you

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: một thuật toán, dù tinh vi đến đâu, cũng không thể dễ dàng tái tạo

chậm hơn, nhỏ hơn, và có lẽ không kinh tế

sampleAnnotations.phrase: a book you did not know to ask for
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: một cuốn sách bạn không biết để hỏi tới

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect for setting the background of a story
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I had told him nothing about myself' và 'He had also been doing this work for forty years' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành ('had + past participle' và 'had been + -ing') để mô tả các hành động đã xảy ra trước thời điểm chính trong quá khứ. Đây là kỹ thuật quan trọng để kể chuyện ở B1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I had told him nothing about myself' and 'He had also been doing this work for forty years' use past perfect ('had + past participle' and past perfect continuous 'had been + -ing') for actions that happened before the main past moment in the story — a key narrative technique at B1.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Defining relative clause without a pronoun (contact clause)
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'a Hungarian writer I had never heard of' bỏ qua đại từ quan hệ 'whom/that' vì người đó là tân ngữ trong mệnh đề. Cấu trúc rút gọn này rất tự nhiên trong tiếng Anh B1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'a Hungarian writer I had never heard of' omits the relative pronoun 'whom/that' because the writer is the object of the relative clause. This reduced ('contact') relative clause is very natural in B1 English.

sampleTranslationVi:
Vào một chiều tháng Mười mưa ở Lisbon, tôi bước vào một hiệu sách cũ nhỏ xíu trên đường Rua das Salgadeiras mà không có ý tưởng rõ ràng về điều tôi đang tìm. Người chủ, một người đàn ông ngoài bảy mươi tên Joaquim, nhìn tôi lướt qua các kệ gần hai mươi phút mà không nói gì. Cuối cùng ông đứng dậy, đi đến một kệ tôi đã đi qua hai lần, và đưa cho tôi một cuốn tiểu thuyết mỏng của một nhà văn Hungary mà tôi chưa từng nghe đến. Ông chỉ nói đơn giản, 'Tôi nghĩ hôm nay đây là cuốn sách dành cho bạn.' Tôi chưa nói với ông điều gì về bản thân. Tôi thậm chí chưa trả tiền gì cả. Vậy mà cuốn sách — về một dịch giả trở về một thành phố cô chưa thăm từ thời thiếu niên — đặt vào tay tôi vào một thời điểm tôi đã suy nghĩ về gần như chính chủ đề đó trong nhiều tuần.

Điều khiến lời gợi ý đó có thể được đưa ra là điều mà một thuật toán, dù tinh vi đến đâu, cũng không thể dễ dàng tái tạo: Joaquim đã quan sát tôi trong hai mươi phút. Ông đã để ý tôi nán lại ở những kệ nào, tôi chạm vào và đặt lại những gáy sách nào, cách tôi cầm một cuốn sách trước khi quyết định không lấy. Ông cũng đã làm công việc này trong bốn mươi năm, ở cùng một khu phố, với cùng loại khách hàng. Mặc dù một công cụ gợi ý biết bạn đã mua gì, nó không thấy vai bạn thả lỏng khi bạn tìm thấy một kiểu bìa nhất định. Nó không ghi nhận việc bạn mở một cuốn sách ra giữa và đọc trong ba mươi giây trước khi đặt nó xuống thất vọng. Nó chỉ vận hành từ dấu vết của những quyết định bạn đã đưa ra, và hầu hết các quyết định đó không phải là những quyết định thú vị.

Điều tôi đã dần hiểu, vài năm sau, là những lời gợi ý tốt nhất không phải là dự đoán điều bạn đã muốn — chúng là những giới thiệu nhẹ nhàng đến điều bạn chưa biết mình muốn. Joaquim đưa cho tôi một cuốn sách mà tôi sẽ không bao giờ tìm kiếm, và một thuật toán theo dõi lịch sử đọc của tôi cũng sẽ không gợi ý nó, bởi vì không gì trong các lần mua trước của tôi chỉ về hướng đó. Tuy nhiên, hiệu sách đã sống nhờ loại phán đoán này cho đến khi nó đóng cửa vào năm 2022. Tôi vẫn nghĩ về nó vào những buổi chiều khi tôi thấy mình lướt qua một danh sách các tựa được gợi ý mà bằng cách nào đó tất cả trông giống nhau. Một cửa hàng với một người dành sự chú ý cho một khách hàng tại một thời điểm thì chậm hơn, nhỏ hơn, và có lẽ không kinh tế. Đôi khi, đó cũng là điều duy nhất có thể cho bạn một cuốn sách bạn không biết để hỏi tới.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past perfect correctly?
options: I had tell him nothing about myself.
I had told him nothing about myself.
I have told him nothing about myself.
I had been told him nothing about myself.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'; the past participle of 'tell' is 'told'. Option C is present perfect, which does not fit a narrative set in the past.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'; 'told' is the past participle of 'tell'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a contact (reduced) relative clause correctly?
options: A Hungarian writer who I had never heard.
A Hungarian writer whom I had never heard about of.
A Hungarian writer I had never heard of.
A Hungarian writer that I had never heard him of.
mcQuestions.explanation: The relative pronoun can be omitted when it is the object of the clause. The preposition 'of' stays in its natural place; 'heard of' is the correct collocation.
mcQuestions.hint: You can drop the object relative pronoun; keep the preposition at the end of the phrasal verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'however' correctly as a mid-sentence concessive?
options: An algorithm, however it is sophisticated, cannot easily reproduce this kind of judgement.
An algorithm, however sophisticatedly, cannot easily reproduce this kind of judgement.
An algorithm, however to be sophisticated, cannot easily reproduce this kind of judgement.
An algorithm, however sophisticated, cannot easily reproduce this kind of judgement.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'However + adjective' is a compact mid-sentence concessive equivalent to 'no matter how sophisticated'. No copula or adverb form is required.
mcQuestions.hint: 'However + adjective' = 'no matter how + adjective'; no verb is added.

options: Although a recommendation engine knows what you have bought, it does not see your shoulders relax.
Although a recommendation engine knows what you have bought, but it does not see your shoulders relax.
Although, a recommendation engine knows what you have bought it does not see your shoulders relax.
Although a recommendation engine know what you have bought, it does not see your shoulders relax.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma directly after 'although', and the verb must agree ('engine knows').
mcQuestions.hint: Never pair 'although' with 'but'; ensure third-person singular agreement.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses the present perfect for reflection over time?
options: What I came to understand, several years later, is that the best recommendations are gentle introductions.
What I have come to understand, several years later, is that the best recommendations are gentle introductions.
What I have came to understand, several years later, is that the best recommendations are gentle introductions.
What I have come understanding, several years later, is that the best recommendations are gentle introductions.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have come' uses the past participle 'come' (irregular, same form as base). 'Have came' is wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: The past participle of 'come' is 'come', not 'came'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'would' correctly to express a past hypothetical?
options: He handed me a book I would never had searched for.
He handed me a book I will never have searched for.
He handed me a book I would never have searched for.
He handed me a book I would never have search for.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would have + past participle' is the structure for past hypothetical results; 'searched' is the past participle of 'search'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Would have + past participle' for hypothetical past outcomes.

options: He noticed which shelves I lingered on at.
He noticed which shelves I lingered in.
He noticed which shelves I was linger at.
He noticed which shelves I lingered at.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Linger at + a place' is the correct collocation; 'lingered at' is past simple, regular.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Linger at + place' (e.g., a shelf, a doorway) is the standard collocation.

options: The way I held a book before deciding against it.
The way I held a book before deciding against to it.
The way I held a book before deciding it against.
The way I held a book before to decide against it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Decide against + noun/pronoun/gerund' is the correct structure meaning 'choose not to'. 'Before + -ing' is also correct.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Decide against' takes a noun/gerund directly; 'before + -ing' is the natural pattern.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'work only from' correctly?
options: It works only from the trail of decisions you already have made.
It works only from the trail of decisions you have already made.
It works only from the trail of decisions you have already make.
It works only from the trail of decisions you have made already since.
mcQuestions.explanation: Mid-position 'already' goes between 'have' and the past participle; the past participle of 'make' is 'made'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Already' typically sits between the auxiliary and the past participle.

options: I find myself to scroll through a list of recommended titles.
I find myself scroll through a list of recommended titles.
I find myself scrolling through a list of recommended titles.
I find me scrolling through a list of recommended titles.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Find oneself + -ing' is the fixed structure for noticing an unintentional behaviour; reflexive 'myself', not 'me'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Find + reflexive + -ing'; the reflexive pronoun is 'myself', not 'me'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'point in a direction' correctly?
options: Nothing about my past purchases pointed to that direction.
Nothing about my past purchases pointed at that direction.
Nothing about my past purchases pointed for that direction.
Nothing about my past purchases pointed in that direction.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Point in a direction' is the correct collocation; 'to', 'at', and 'for' do not fit before 'direction' in this metaphorical sense.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Point in + direction' is the fixed collocation in this figurative sense.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If a small bookshop's recommendation can introduce you to something you would never have searched for, while an algorithm only refines what you already want, is there value in deliberately seeking sources that do not know you well?
